content.claim: Established economic research (Kertesi–Kézdi) demonstrates that Hungary's free-school-choice regime combined with residential/commuting mobility drives school segregation and cross-boundary sorting by family status, establishing a pre-existing structural condition into which rising SNI identification enters.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Implementation planning lens
limitations: The general segregation mechanism is well-established, but no quantitative study directly measuring SNI-classification-driven parental mobility was located
The specific interaction between rising SNI identification and school-choice sorting remains an evidentiary gap requiring dedicated study
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
